First, a validated finite difference code (e.g., FLAC) will model a suite of representative liquefiable soil profiles to generate time histories of ground deformation, isolating components of uniform settlement, differential settlement (characterized by angular distortion), and lateral extension (characterized by ground strain). Second, these deformation time series will be applied as displacement boundary conditions to detailed nonlinear finite element models of representative reinforced concrete and steel moment-frame buildings in OpenSees. Damage will be quantified using engineering demand parameters (e.g., inter-story drift, column curvature ductility) and correlated with repair cost models from pre-2026 literature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative basis for ranking the severity of different post-liquefaction ground failure modes on structures. The results will directly inform revisions to performance-based earthquake engineering practice and could lead to proposals for code amendments in TS 1170.5 or related standards, specifically for integrating differential deformation hazards into site-specific assessment.
proposals.novelty: While soil-structure interaction under uniform settlement is studied, this proposal systematically decouples and compares the structural effects of differential settlement and lateral extension—deformation modes prevalent in liquefaction but often conflated in analysis—using a direct, displacement-controlled numerical framework aligned with modern performance-based engineering principles.

proposals.title: A Probabilistic Liquefaction Hazard Mapping Framework for Port Areas: Integrating Site-Specific Fill History and Nonlinear Site Response
proposals.motivation: Ports are critical infrastructure hubs often built on reclaimed land with highly variable and undocumented fill materials, making them exceptionally vulnerable to liquefaction during seismic events. Existing regional liquefaction susceptibility maps lack the resolution and geotechnical specificity needed for port asset management and resilience planning. This research addresses a critical gap in applying the latest site classification and nonlinear site-response methodologies from standards like TS 1170.5 to these complex, high-consequence environments.
proposals.hypothesis: That a probabilistic liquefaction hazard analysis (PLHA) framework, which explicitly incorporates spatially variable fill characteristics and construction-era-specific engineering properties, will yield significantly more accurate and spatially refined hazard maps for port areas than current methods based on generalized surficial geology.
proposals.method: The method will integrate geospatial, geotechnical, and seismic data. First, a high-resolution geotechnical database for the port area will be constructed by digitizing historical construction plans, borehole logs, and conducting new CPTu soundings to characterize fill types, ages, and densities. This database will inform the development of a 3D subsurface model. Site-specific seismic input motions will be derived using the NZS/TS 1170.5 framework, adjusted for nonlinear site-response effects using PGA adjustment factors as per Cubrinovski's recent work. The PLHA will be performed using the Boulanger & Idriss (2014) CPT-based procedure within a Monte Carlo simulation framework, propagating uncertainties in soil properties and ground motions to compute probabilistic liquefaction triggering hazard curves at grid points across the port.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, practical methodology for generating port-specific liquefaction hazard maps that directly inform seismic design (TS 1170.5) and retrofit prioritization. It will advance the field by demonstrating a fully probabilistic, data-integrated approach for high-stakes, heterogeneous sites. The resulting open-source framework and case-study maps will provide a template for port authorities globally.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of detailed historical fill characterization with the latest NZ seismic code's site-response procedures within a high-resolution PLHA framework, moving beyond generic mapping to asset-specific, mechanics-based hazard quantification for port environments.

proposals.title: Quantifying Port-Wide Liquefaction-Induced Settlements Using Multi-Temporal InSAR: A Framework for Seismic Performance Assessment and TS 1170.5 Site Classification
proposals.motivation: Ports are critical infrastructure hubs often built on soft, liquefiable soils, making them highly vulnerable to permanent ground deformation during earthquakes. Pre-2026 seismic design codes like NZS/TS 1170.5 rely on simplified site classification and empirical factors to estimate these effects, but lack direct, high-resolution measurements of pre- and post-seismic deformation at the scale of an entire port facility. This gap limits the validation and refinement of design provisions for these complex, engineered sites.
proposals.hypothesis: Time-series InSAR analysis can reliably detect and quantify spatially variable, liquefaction-induced ground settlement and lateral spreading at port facilities, and these measurements will reveal systematic relationships between deformation patterns, local soil conditions, and port infrastructure performance that are not captured by current code-based site classification methods.
proposals.method: The research will employ multi-temporal InSAR (MT-InSAR) techniques, specifically Persistent Scatterer (PS) and Small Baseline Subset (SBAS) approaches, on C-band (Sentinel-1) and L-band (ALOS-2/PALSAR-2) SAR data stacks. We will process pre- and post-earthquake data for selected New Zealand port areas using established software (e.g., GMTSAR, StaMPS/MTI). The derived line-of-sight displacement time series will be decomposed into vertical and east-west components. Results will be correlated with high-resolution geotechnical data (CPT logs, boreholes) and compared against deformation predictions from simplified models implicit in TS 1170.5.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first port-wide, data-driven benchmarks for liquefaction-induced deformation, directly contributing to the performance-based assessment of port infrastructure. It will deliver a novel methodology for validating and potentially refining the site-specific input motions and amplification factors within the TS 1170.5 framework for port environments.
proposals.novelty: While InSAR has been used for regional subsidence studies, its targeted application to decompose and quantify the specific components of seismic deformation (settlement vs. lateral spread) across the heterogeneous, engineered fills and natural deposits of a port is novel. Integrating these measurements directly with code-based site response and hazard analysis procedures represents a significant advance.

proposals.title: A Forensic Engineering Framework for Distinguishing Liquefaction-Induced Damage from Other Seismic Damage Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Post-earthquake damage assessments often struggle to definitively attribute foundation and ground failure to soil liquefaction versus other causes like inertial shaking or lateral spreading without pore pressure rise. This ambiguity complicates insurance claims, code development, and retrofit strategies. With the recent update to TS 1170.5:2025 providing new site classification and nonlinear site response provisions, a systematic forensic methodology is needed to validate these models against observed damage and improve future design.
proposals.hypothesis: A systematic forensic engineering protocol, integrating geotechnical, structural, and seismological data, can reliably distinguish damage primarily caused by liquefaction (e.g., loss of soil strength, post-liquefaction settlement) from damage caused by strong ground shaking (e.g., inertial structural failure) or non-liquefaction ground deformation.
proposals.method: The research will develop a decision-tree framework based on forensic indicators. It will integrate: 1) Site-specific ground motion analysis using TS 1170.5:2025 methods to compute predicted site amplification and compare with recorded motions; 2) Detailed CPT-based liquefaction triggering and consequence analysis (e.g., using the Boulanger & Idriss 2014 method) to estimate spatial patterns of strength loss and settlement; 3) Structural analysis to assess whether observed damage patterns (e.g., differential settlement, tilting) are consistent with calculated ground deformation or with inertial demands from the site-specific shaking. Case studies from the 2010-2011 Canterbury Earthquake Sequence will be used to develop and test the protocol.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a standardized, evidence-based protocol for forensic earthquake engineering, directly linking observed damage to specific geotechnical failure mechanisms. It will offer a critical validation tool for the site response and hazard models in TS 1170.5, helping to refine site classification and nonlinear adjustment factors for liquefaction-prone areas.
proposals.novelty: While liquefaction assessment methods exist, a formalized forensic engineering framework to *distinguish* liquefaction damage from other seismic damage causes in post-event reconnaissance is novel. It moves beyond identification to systematic root-cause analysis, explicitly integrating the latest NZ design code provisions.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Geomorphic and Geotechnical Controls on Liquefaction Manifestation: A Test of the 'Major Waterway' Hypothesis for Christchurch and Wellington
proposals.motivation: Post-2010-2011 Canterbury earthquake sequence observations in Christchurch, New Zealand, suggested surface liquefaction effects were strongly concentrated near major rivers and streams. This empirical pattern challenges conventional liquefaction hazard mapping, which often uses regional geologic units or simplified groundwater models. Validating and quantifying this geomorphic control is critical for updating site classification and seismic design practice, as reflected in the recent evolution of standards like NZS/TS 1170.5, to better account for localized high-hazard zones.
proposals.hypothesis: Surface manifestation of seismically induced liquefaction is primarily confined to geologically recent (Holocene) fluvial deposits within a specific, quantifiable distance (e.g., < 500 m) of active river channels and major paleochannels, due to the combined controls of youthful, loose sedimentation and persistently high groundwater levels.
proposals.method: The research will integrate high-resolution geomorphic mapping (using pre-2026 LiDAR and historic maps) with an extensive geotechnical database from Christchurch and new case studies in Wellington. We will statistically test the correlation between liquefaction manifestation points (from post-earthquake reconnaissance) and distance-to-major-waterway using logistic regression. The analysis will control for seismic demand (using NZS 1170.5-based input motions) and soil profile data (CPT). A key step is developing a new 'fluvial influence' parameter for site classification, potentially modifying the 'site class' factor in TS 1170.5 for areas meeting specific geomorphic and geotechnical criteria.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, data-driven framework for incorporating geomorphic setting into liquefaction hazard assessment. It will directly inform updates to NZ geotechnical practice and seismic design standards by proposing a supplementary site classification criterion. The results will enable more targeted and cost-effective ground improvement and planning regulations by refining hazard zones beyond regional geology.
proposals.novelty: While the spatial pattern is empirically noted, this study formally tests and quantifies the 'waterway proximity' hypothesis using advanced geospatial statistics and integrates it directly with the site classification methodology of a national seismic design standard (TS 1170.5), moving from observation to codifiable practice.

proposals.title: Quantifying Post-Liquefaction Stiffness Degradation and Recovery Using High-Resolution Site Characterization for Improved Ground Motion Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design standards, such as TS 1170.5, incorporate site amplification factors based on pre-event soil properties. However, liquefaction-induced ground failure fundamentally alters the stiffness and layering of soil deposits, rendering pre-event site classifications and associated design spectra potentially non-conservative for aftershocks or future events. There is a critical need to understand the post-liquefaction state of soil to inform recovery-phase assessments and long-term seismic hazard.
proposals.hypothesis: Detailed post-liquefaction site investigations will reveal systematic, quantifiable reductions in shear wave velocity and penetration resistance, and that the spatial variability of this degradation can be correlated with the severity of surface manifestations to update site class and nonlinear site-response parameters.
proposals.method: Following a major seismic event with observed liquefaction, we will conduct targeted investigations at sites with well-documented pre-event Vs profiles. The methodology employs a multi-tool approach: Dynamic Cone Penetration Tests (DCPT) for high-resolution, near-surface strength profiling; Seismic While Drilling (SWD) or Suspension P-S Logging (SPS) for direct measurement of post-liquefaction Vs with depth; and Spectral Analysis of Surface Waves (SASW) for 2D profiling of stiffness recovery over time. Data will be integrated to create updated soil profiles, which will be used as input for 1D nonlinear site-response analyses (using codes like DEEPSOIL or OpenSees) to compute revised site amplification factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first comprehensive datasets linking post-liquefaction geotechnical properties to in-situ test measurements. It will yield empirical relationships for stiffness degradation ratios and recovery rates. The results will directly inform potential amendments to seismic design codes, like TS 1170.5, for post-liquefaction scenarios, moving practice from pre-event assumptions to state-aware hazard assessment.
proposals.novelty: While post-event reconnaissance is common, this proposal systematically quantifies the *in-situ* geotechnical state after liquefaction using a combination of tools not typically deployed together in emergency response, focusing on generating direct inputs for site-response analysis and code development rather than solely on failure documentation.

proposals.title: A Framework for Updating Regional Liquefaction Susceptibility Maps Using Site-Specific Nonlinear Response and Modern Seismic Hazard Inputs
proposals.motivation: Current regional liquefaction susceptibility maps in New Zealand and similar seismic environments often rely on simplified, outdated site classification and ground motion inputs. The recent updates to the national seismic design standard (TS 1170.5:2025) incorporate advanced site-response methodologies and nonlinear PGA adjustment factors, creating a disconnect with existing hazard maps. This research aims to bridge that gap, providing a methodology to translate site-specific lessons into updated, more reliable regional-scale maps for improved land-use planning and risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically integrating the site classification framework and nonlinear PGA adjustment factors from TS 1170.5:2025 with regionally available geotechnical and geospatial data, a scalable, physics-informed methodology can be developed to produce updated liquefaction susceptibility maps that better reflect modern understanding of site-specific seismic response.
proposals.method: The method will leverage the site classification logic and nonlinear PGA adjustment procedures from TS 1170.5:2025 as a core engine. We will develop a GIS-based workflow that ingests regional-scale data layers (e.g., VS30 from microzonation studies, groundwater levels, geologic age, CPT soundings) to classify sites across a region according to the TS 1170.5 taxonomy. For each classified unit, appropriate nonlinear scaling factors will be applied to probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) ground motions to derive 'site-adjusted' shaking demands. These will be combined with simplified liquefaction triggering models (e.g., Boulanger & Idriss, 2014) to compute a regional Liquefaction Potential Index (LPI). The final output will be a probabilistic liquefaction susceptibility map at a regional scale (e.g., 1:25,000).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a transparent, standards-aligned framework for updating legacy liquefaction maps, directly linking modern code-based site response practice to regional hazard assessment. It will deliver a prototype updated map for a test region (e.g., Canterbury or Wellington), demonstrating the impact of incorporating nonlinear site effects. The methodology will be a template for other jurisdictions adopting similar advanced site classification in their seismic codes.
proposals.novelty: While site-specific nonlinear analysis is established, its systematic, code-informed application to *regional* liquefaction mapping is novel. This approach moves beyond generic VS30 proxies by explicitly incorporating the formal site classification and adjustment factor logic of a contemporary national design standard into a scalable geographic framework.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Reset of Liquefaction Potential Between Seismic Events: A Framework for Multi-Shock Hazard Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design and liquefaction hazard assessment, as codified in standards like NZS/TS 1170.5, typically treat major aftershocks as independent events, assuming soil properties and pore pressure conditions revert to pre-mainshock states. This assumption is conservative but may be physically unrealistic, potentially leading to inaccurate predictions of cumulative damage in seismic sequences like those observed in Canterbury. A physics-based understanding of inter-event soil recovery is critical for refining multi-hazard assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The liquefaction potential of a soil deposit does not fully reset to its pre-mainshock state before a significant aftershock; instead, the post-mainshock state, characterized by residual pore pressures and altered soil structure, provides the initial conditions for subsequent shaking.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled numerical framework integrating 1D nonlinear site-response analysis (e.g., using DEEPSOIL or OpenSees) with advanced constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand) capable of tracking pore pressure generation and dissipation. We will simulate canonical soil profiles from NZ site databases through mainshock-aftershock sequences. The key analysis will invert the standard assumption by initiating the aftershock analysis from the end-state of the mainshock simulation, rather than from a 'reset' initial state. Statistical comparison of predicted liquefaction manifestation (e.g., factor of safety, strain accumulation) between the reset and non-reset assumptions will be performed for a suite of recorded and synthetic sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanics-based framework for assessing cumulative liquefaction hazard in seismic sequences. It will deliver practical guidance on when the 'full reset' assumption is non-conservative and propose a methodology for incorporating sequential shaking effects into next-generation site-specific hazard analyses, potentially informing future revisions to NZ geotechnical practice.
proposals.novelty: While post-liquefaction reconsolidation is studied, explicitly inverting the standard engineering assumption of full reset for design-level hazard analysis of aftershocks is a novel and critical step towards more realistic time-dependent hazard assessment.

proposals.title: A Framework for Seismic Liquefaction Hazard Assessment Integrating Rupture Depth and Directivity into Ground Motion Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction hazard assessments, including those underpinning codes like TS 1170.5, often rely on epicentral distance as the primary spatial metric. This simplification can misrepresent the intensity of shaking at a site, as it neglects the effects of finite fault rupture depth and directivity, which strongly influence near-source ground motions. A more physically accurate spatial metric is needed to improve the reliability of liquefaction triggering evaluations, especially for critical infrastructure in seismically active regions like New Zealand.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating rupture depth and directivity effects into ground motion prediction, moving beyond simple epicentral distance, will significantly improve the accuracy of predicted seismic demands used in liquefaction hazard assessment. This refined demand will lead to a more spatially precise and physically realistic estimation of liquefaction risk.
proposals.method: The research will develop a probabilistic framework that modifies existing ground motion models (GMMs) used in TS 1170.5. For a suite of scenario earthquakes, we will replace epicentral distance with rupture distance (R_rup) and incorporate directivity parameters (e.g., the directivity predictor of Shahi & Baker, 2014). The modified seismic demands will be input into established liquefaction triggering procedures (e.g., Boulanger & Idriss, 2014). The methodology will be tested and calibrated using case histories from well-documented earthquakes (e.g., 2010-2011 Canterbury, 2016 Kaikōura) where depth and directivity effects were pronounced.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical methodology for integrating key source effects into code-oriented liquefaction hazard assessment. It will deliver adjustment factors or a supplementary framework for TS 1170.5 practitioners to refine site-specific demands. The outcome is a more robust link between seismic source characterization and geotechnical failure risk.
proposals.novelty: While source effects are studied in seismology, their systematic integration into standard-of-practice liquefaction hazard assessment, particularly within the context of the newly released TS 1170.5 code, represents a novel advancement. The proposal moves beyond advocating for complex physics-based simulations to provide an engineering-implementable method.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Preconditioning Effect of the 2010 Darfield Earthquake on Liquefaction Severity during the 2011 Christchurch Aftershock: A Site-Specific Cumulative Damage Analysis
proposals.motivation: The 2010-2011 Canterbury earthquake sequence demonstrated that liquefaction damage can escalate dramatically in aftershocks, challenging conventional seismic design which treats events in isolation. Current standards like NZS/TS 1170.5 focus on single-event hazards and lack explicit guidance for cumulative damage from sequences. Understanding this mainshock-aftershock (MS-AS) soil preconditioning is critical for improving the resilience of infrastructure in seismically active regions.
proposals.hypothesis: The 2010 Darfield mainshock induced partial pore pressure generation and soil fabric changes that preconditioned Christchurch soils, significantly lowering the resistance to liquefaction triggering and amplifying its severity during the 2011 aftershock, beyond what would be predicted by considering the aftershock motions alone.
proposals.method: The study will employ a coupled, site-specific numerical framework. First, a high-resolution geotechnical model of key Christchurch sites will be developed using CPT and SPT data. The 2010 mainshock ground motions will be applied using nonlinear effective-stress site response analysis (e.g., using DEEPSOIL or OpenSees with PM4Sand/PM4Silt constitutive models) to simulate the post-mainshock pore pressure and strain state. This residual state will serve as the initial condition for the subsequent analysis of the 2011 aftershock motions. The cumulative damage (e.g., excess pore pressure ratio, shear strain) from the sequence will be quantified and compared against a benchmark analysis of the 2011 motions applied to a 'virgin' soil state.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanics-based assessment of MS-AS liquefaction damage, directly informing potential updates to seismic design practice in NZS/TS 1170.5. It will deliver site-specific insights into the Canterbury sequence and propose a practical analytical framework for evaluating cumulative liquefaction hazard, a critical step towards performance-based earthquake engineering for sequential events.
proposals.novelty: While post-2011 studies documented the sequence's effects, few have rigorously modeled the full stress-strain-pore pressure path from the 2010 through the 2011 event for real Christchurch soil profiles using advanced constitutive models, explicitly linking the documented site preconditioning to quantifiable changes in soil state and response.

proposals.title: A Geomorphic-Unit-Based Framework for Predicting Liquefaction Manifestation: Integrating TS 1170.5 Site Classification with Detailed Landform Mapping
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design standards, such as TS 1170.5, classify sites based on near-surface shear-wave velocity (Vs30) and soil type, but these parameters may not fully capture the spatial variability of liquefaction susceptibility within a site class. Geomorphic units (e.g., abandoned channels, point bars, wetlands) are known to control the depositional history and geotechnical properties of soils, yet this knowledge is not systematically integrated into probabilistic liquefaction hazard assessment. A methodology linking observed liquefaction performance to specific geomorphic units would significantly refine site-specific hazard characterization.
proposals.hypothesis: That the probability of surface liquefaction manifestation for a given seismic demand (e.g., PGA from TS 1170.5) can be more accurately predicted by classifying sites according to detailed geomorphic units than by using Vs30-based site classes alone.
proposals.method: The research will compile a high-resolution geomorphic map (e.g., using LiDAR-derived digital elevation models and historic aerial imagery) for case-study areas in New Zealand with well-documented liquefaction from past earthquakes (e.g., 2010-2011 Canterbury sequence). For each mapped geomorphic unit (e.g., active channel, abandoned channel, floodplain, swamp deposit), we will extract and statistically analyze correlated parameters: CPT/SPT-based liquefaction triggering indices, Vs30 measurements, and groundwater data. A Bayesian logistic regression model will be developed to predict the probability of liquefaction manifestation as a function of TS 1170.5-adjusted PGA and geomorphic unit classification, comparing its performance against a baseline model using only site class.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, map-based framework that directly integrates geomorphic context into liquefaction hazard evaluation, complementing the TS 1170.5 site classification system. It will provide engineers and planners with spatially explicit likelihoods of liquefaction for land-use planning and site investigation prioritization. The resulting correlation matrices between geomorphic units and liquefaction performance indices will be a valuable resource for future seismic standards development.
proposals.novelty: While the influence of depositional environment on liquefaction is acknowledged, this study proposes a quantitative, probabilistic link between standardized seismic hazard inputs (TS 1170.5) and detailed geomorphic mapping for prediction, moving beyond qualitative geomorphic checklists to a statistically rigorous, implementable model.

proposals.title: Quantifying Soil Fabric Evolution During Liquefaction Using X-ray Computed Tomography: Implications for Site-Specific Ground Motion Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design standards, like TS 1170.5, rely on simplified site classification and linear-equivalent PGA adjustments, which inadequately capture the complex, nonlinear effects of liquefaction on ground motions. The fundamental link between the microscale sediment fabric, its evolution during cyclic loading, and the resulting macroscale dynamic response remains poorly quantified, limiting the accuracy of site-specific hazard assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The application of medical-grade X-ray computed tomography (CT) to undisturbed soil cores will reveal distinct, quantifiable changes in particle arrangement, pore structure, and density distribution pre- and post-liquefaction, and these fabric metrics will correlate with measured changes in shear wave velocity and cyclic resistance.
proposals.method: Undisturbed samples of liquefiable silts and sands will be obtained from high-risk Christchurch sites. Each sample will undergo high-resolution (sub-100 micron) CT scanning before and after controlled cyclic simple shear testing in a geotechnical centrifuge or laminar box. The 3D image stacks will be processed using digital image correlation (DIC) and segmentation algorithms (e.g., watershed, machine learning-based in Avizo or ImageJ) to extract fabric descriptors: coordination number, particle orientation, and pore network morphology. These descriptors will be statistically correlated with pre- and post-test Vs measurements from bender elements and cyclic stress ratios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, 3D empirical evidence of fabric change due to liquefaction, moving beyond inferred indices. The results will inform the development of next-generation, microstructure-informed adjustment factors for nonlinear site response in design codes and improve the physical basis for site classification in liquefaction-prone areas.
proposals.novelty: While CT scanning has been used in geotechnics, its application as a direct, quantitative tool to track the *evolution* of in-situ soil fabric through a full liquefaction cycle, and to link this to engineering design parameters, is a novel and transformative approach.

proposals.title: A Network Epidemiology Model for the Spatial Propagation of Liquefaction-Induced Pipeline Damage in Urban Areas
proposals.motivation: Current infrastructure damage models for liquefaction often treat damage at individual sites as independent, failing to capture the cascading, spatially-correlated nature of failure in networked systems like water or gas pipelines. This limits the accuracy of post-earthquake serviceability assessments and resilience planning. Integrating the spatial propagation of ground failure with network vulnerability is a critical, unresolved challenge in geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial spread of pipeline damage due to liquefaction can be effectively modeled using modified epidemiological frameworks (e.g., SIR or network contagion models), where the 'infection' represents the transition of a pipeline segment from an intact to a damaged state, driven by spatially-dependent seismic ground motion and soil susceptibility.
proposals.method: The method will integrate pre-2026 geotechnical and epidemiological models. First, a high-resolution liquefaction hazard map will be generated for a case-study area using NZS 1170.5:2004/TS 1170.5 methodologies to define site-specific 'susceptibility'. This map will define the initial 'infected' nodes (pipe segments where liquefaction is triggered). The pipeline network will be represented as a graph. A stochastic Susceptible-Infected (SI) or Independent Cascade model will be adapted, where the probability of a pipe segment (node) failing is a function of its own liquefaction potential and the state of neighboring segments, incorporating factors like hydraulic interdependence and mechanical coupling. Calibration and validation will use empirical damage data from past earthquakes like the 2010-2011 Canterbury sequence.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, computationally efficient framework for predicting the spatial clustering and propagation of infrastructure damage, moving beyond point-based fragility curves. It will provide utility operators and urban planners with a new tool for identifying critical network vulnerabilities and planning targeted retrofits to inhibit damage spread, thereby enhancing systemic resilience.
proposals.novelty: While network theory has been applied to infrastructure, the explicit adaptation of dynamic epidemiological contagion models to simulate the physical spread of geotechnically-driven damage is novel. It directly links the spatial statistics of ground failure (Cubrinovski's core theme) with network failure progression in a way that standard seismic risk models do not.

proposals.title: A Forensic Engineering Framework for Liquefaction-Induced Foundation Failures: Case Studies and Implications for NZS 1170.5 Site Classification
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in liquefaction hazard assessment, significant discrepancies persist between predicted and observed foundation performance during major earthquakes. These failures represent a critical gap in validating and refining current design codes like NZS 1170.5. A systematic, forensic analysis of specific failure cases is needed to move beyond generic models and understand the complex site-foundation-seismic motion interactions that lead to collapse.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a structured forensic engineering methodology to documented liquefaction-induced foundation failures will reveal specific, quantifiable deficiencies in current site classification and nonlinear site-response procedures, particularly for marginal sites not fully captured by TS 1170.5:2025.
proposals.method: The research will employ a modified version of the Failure Analysis and Risk Management (FARM) framework. For 3-5 well-documented case histories (e.g., from the 2010-2011 Canterbury Earthquake Sequence), we will: 1) Reconstruct the pre-event ground model using all available site investigation data; 2) Back-analyze the seismic demand using recorded motions and site-response analysis (e.g., DEEPSOIL or equivalent 1D nonlinear codes); 3) Apply the TS 1170.5:2025 site classification and PGA adjustment factor methodology to the reconstructed site; and 4) Compare the code-predicted performance with the forensic evidence of actual foundation failure mechanisms (e.g., bearing failure, excessive settlement, lateral spreading).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated forensic protocol for geotechnical earthquake engineering failures. It will provide direct, evidence-based feedback on the limitations and potential improvements for the site classification and nonlinear adjustment factors in NZS 1170.5, moving the code towards more performance-based foundations. The case study database will serve as a benchmark for validating advanced numerical models.
proposals.novelty: While forensic studies exist, this proposal systematically integrates the detailed forensic process with the specific, newly updated procedures of TS 1170.5:2025, creating a direct feedback loop from physical failure to code provision. It treats the failure site not just as a case history but as a full-scale, natural experiment to test the code's predictive capability.

proposals.title: Delineating Critical Thresholds for Liquefaction Triggering: A Comparative Case Study of Geologically Similar Liquefied and Non-Liquefied Sites in Christchurch
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction hazard assessments, including those underpinning seismic design standards like TS 1170.5, rely on simplified site classification and ground motion parameters (e.g., PGA) that may not fully capture the complex, nonlinear soil response leading to liquefaction. The 2010-2011 Canterbury Earthquake Sequence provides a unique natural laboratory, with well-documented zones of severe liquefaction adjacent to geologically similar areas that did not liquefy. Systematically studying these 'paired' cases is critical for identifying the specific combinations of soil state, stratigraphy, and dynamic excitation that control triggering, moving beyond probabilistic correlations to more physically-based models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within geologically similar depositional environments, the occurrence or non-occurrence of liquefaction during the Canterbury earthquakes can be explained by distinct, quantifiable thresholds in a combined parameter space of soil state (e.g., density, stress history), stratigraphic confinement, and frequency-dependent seismic energy (e.g., Arias Intensity, duration) that are not captured by PGA alone.
proposals.method: The study will select 3-5 paired areas in Christchurch (liquefied vs. non-liquefied) using pre-2026 geotechnical databases (e.g., CPT logs, SPT data, shear wave velocity profiles) and geological maps to ensure similarity in depositional setting and age. For each pair, we will reconstruct detailed 2D cross-sections and compute state parameters (e.g., state parameter ξ, overburden stress-corrected penetration resistances (qc1N, (N1)60cs)). We will then apply pre-2026 constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand) in 1D nonlinear site-response analyses (using software like DEEPSOIL or OpenSees) driven by multiple recorded ground motions from the events. The analysis will systematically vary input motion characteristics to identify the critical combinations of intensity measures (PGA, Ia, duration) and soil state that trigger liquefaction in the models, comparing directly with the field observations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, mechanics-based framework for explaining liquefaction triggering at a site-specific scale, directly informing the development of next-generation site classification and adjustment factors for seismic codes. It will yield practical guidance on which ground motion intensity measures and subsurface characterization parameters are most critical for distinguishing high-risk zones within broad geological units, enhancing the precision of microzonation maps.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have analyzed liquefied sites, the deliberate, systematic pairing with geologically analogous non-liquefied cases using high-resolution field data and advanced constitutive modeling to isolate triggering thresholds is a novel and powerful approach. This moves the field from identifying hazardous areas to pinpointing the precise conditions that separate failure from stability.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Dynamic Pore Pressure Changes on Liquefaction Potential: A Framework for Post-Seismic and Seasonal Groundwater Fluctuations
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction assessment procedures, including those in standards like NZS 1170.5, typically assume a static groundwater table (GWT) for design. This simplification ignores rapid post-seismic pore pressure redistribution and seasonal fluctuations, which can significantly alter the effective stress state and liquefaction susceptibility of soils in the hours to days following an earthquake. This gap may lead to non-conservative assessments of post-event stability and infrastructure performance.
proposals.hypothesis: Post-seismic and seasonal groundwater table fluctuations will significantly alter the spatial and temporal evolution of liquefaction potential, with the most critical conditions potentially occurring after the cessation of strong ground shaking, challenging the static GWT assumption.
proposals.method: The research will employ coupled nonlinear effective stress site-response analysis using a platform like OpenSees or FLAC, incorporating a critical state soil constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand). A key innovation will be the sequential application of seismic motion (using TS 1170.5 spectra as input) followed by a transient groundwater flow model to simulate post-shaking pore pressure dissipation and seasonal GWT changes. The methodology will be applied to a matrix of synthetic soil profiles representing typical NZ site classes, with results compared against standard simplified procedures using static GWT.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for assessing time-dependent liquefaction hazard, directly informing potential revisions to code-based assessment methodologies. It will yield practical guidance on when dynamic GWT analysis is warranted and may propose adjustment factors for post-seismic scenarios, extending the nonlinear site-response work foundational to TS 1170.5.
proposals.novelty: While site response and liquefaction triggering are well-studied, the explicit coupling of post-seismic transient pore pressure flow with seismic site-response analysis to evaluate evolving liquefaction potential post-shaking is a novel integration of pre-2026 methods, moving beyond the instantaneous triggering paradigm.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Data Fusion Framework for Probabilistic Identification of Thin Soil Layers Using CPT, MASW, and Borehole Data
proposals.motivation: Accurate site characterization, particularly the identification of thin, low-shear-strength layers, is critical for predicting liquefaction triggering and nonlinear site response, as emphasized in recent updates to seismic design codes like TS 1170.5. Current practice often relies on single investigation methods (e.g., CPT alone), which can miss or mischaracterize thin layers, leading to significant uncertainty in site-specific ground motion prediction and liquefaction hazard assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: A probabilistic data fusion framework that formally integrates Cone Penetration Test (CPT) tip resistance, Multichannel Analysis of Surface Waves (MASW) shear-wave velocity (Vs), and sparse borehole lithology data will provide a more robust and probabilistically quantified identification of thin soil layers than any single method used in isolation.
proposals.method: The framework will be built on a Bayesian updating methodology. A prior subsurface model (e.g., layer thicknesses, Vs, soil behavior type index) will be defined from CPT data using established soil behavior type charts and inversion techniques. This prior will be sequentially updated using likelihood functions derived from MASW dispersion data (via a forward model like the Haskell-Thomson method) and from borehole log observations. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling will be used to estimate the posterior distribution of layer properties, explicitly quantifying uncertainty in layer boundaries and material parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, code-relevant tool for geotechnical engineers to reduce epistemic uncertainty in site models for seismic design. It will directly contribute to more reliable site classification and the selection of appropriate input motions for nonlinear site response analysis, as per the goals of TS 1170.5. The output will be a probabilistic soil profile that quantifies the confidence in thin-layer detection.
proposals.novelty: While CPT-MASW correlations exist, this proposal integrates them within a formal Bayesian inference scheme that also incorporates direct lithological observations to resolve thin layers, moving beyond deterministic or simple weighted-averaging fusion techniques common in pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: Incorporating Stratification and Low Hydraulic Conductivity into Site-Specific Liquefaction Assessment: A Framework for Silty Soils
proposals.motivation: Current simplified liquefaction evaluation procedures, including those informing seismic design codes like TS 1170.5, often assume a uniform, fully drained soil column. This is unrealistic for many silty deposits in New Zealand, which are stratified and have low hydraulic conductivity, leading to complex, partially drained pore pressure response during seismic shaking. This oversimplification can lead to significant mischaracterization of liquefaction hazard, settlement, and lateral spreading potential.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling soil stratification and the low hydraulic conductivity typical of silty sites will produce significantly different estimates of excess pore pressure generation, dissipation, and consequent ground deformations compared to analyses assuming a uniform, perfectly drained deposit.
proposals.method: The research will develop a numerical modeling framework using 1D nonlinear effective-stress site response analysis (e.g., DEEPSOIL, OpenSees) with coupled solid-fluid formulations. Site-specific stratigraphy from CPTu and borehole data will be modeled with layer-specific hydraulic conductivity (k) and soil constitutive models (e.g., PM4Silt). A parametric study will systematically vary layer thickness, k, and silt content, subjecting models to a suite of spectrum-compatible and recorded ground motions. Results will be compared against equivalent uniform-soil analyses to quantify differences in PGA amplification, pore pressure ratio (ru), and post-liquefaction settlement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, evidence-based methodology for moving beyond the uniform soil assumption in code-oriented liquefaction assessment. It will deliver guidance on when stratification and partial drainage must be considered and propose adjustments to site classification or input motion selection protocols for TS 1170.5 to better account for these effects in silty soils.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of partial drainage is recognized in advanced research, its systematic integration into a site-response framework tailored for code-based practice and specifically targeting the stratified nature of common silty deposits represents a significant advancement from pre-2026 simplified methods.

proposals.title: Characterizing Time-Dependent Post-Liquefaction Reconsolidation of Low-Plasticity Silts for Improved Settlement Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design standards, such as NZS/TS 1170.5, focus on immediate seismic demand and site classification but provide limited guidance for predicting post-earthquake ground settlement, particularly for problematic soils like low-plasticity silts. These soils are known to exhibit significant delayed, or time-dependent, reconsolidation after liquefaction, leading to damaging settlements that can occur days to weeks after shaking ceases, posing a major risk to infrastructure. This gap hinders accurate post-event recovery planning and long-term performance assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The time-dependent reconsolidation of low-plasticity silts following liquefaction is governed by a definable relationship between soil index properties, initial cyclic stress ratio, and the dissipation of residual excess pore pressures, which can be modeled to predict the magnitude and rate of delayed settlement.
proposals.method: The research will employ a combined experimental and analytical modeling approach. A series of stress-controlled cyclic direct simple shear (DSS) tests on reconstituted low-plasticity silt specimens will be conducted to induce liquefaction, followed by long-term monitoring of pore pressure dissipation and vertical strain under constant effective stress. The experimental data will be used to calibrate and refine a one-dimensional, time-dependent reconsolidation model that couples a standard consolidation theory framework with a rate law for excess pore pressure decay derived from the test results. The model's predictive capability will be tested against case history data from past earthquakes where silt liquefaction was observed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for predicting the magnitude and timeline of delayed post-liquefaction settlement in low-plasticity silts, directly informing post-earthquake impact assessments. The results will offer critical data and a methodological basis for future updates to geotechnical practice and seismic standards (e.g., TS 1170.5) regarding settlement hazard for problematic soil types.
proposals.novelty: While post-liquefaction settlement in sands is relatively well-studied, this proposal specifically targets the poorly quantified time-dependent behavior of low-plasticity silts, integrating cyclic laboratory testing with a dedicated reconsolidation model to address a significant gap between standard site response analysis and long-term ground performance.

ideas.title: High-Resolution Subsurface Imaging for Foundation-Specific Liquefaction Performance Prediction
ideas.description: This research integrates high-resolution geophysical imaging (e.g., seismic tomography) with detailed forensic analysis of foundation performance to develop a physics-based, site-specific predictive model. The model will correlate fine-scale subsurface heterogeneities—such as thin low-permeability layers or pockets of loose sand—with the distinct damage mechanisms observed in shallow versus deep foundations during liquefaction. The outcome is a performance-based design tool that allows engineers to select and detail foundation systems based on a quantified understanding of the site's 3D vulnerability structure, optimizing resilience and cost-effectiveness for new construction and retrofit projects.

ideas.title: Modeling Multi-Structure Interaction in Liquefied Soil as a Networked Oscillator System
ideas.description: This research expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from coupled oscillator dynamics. The analogous problem—predicting synchronization in systems like firefly swarms or Josephson junction arrays—is solved by modeling individual oscillators and their coupling rules. Adapted to geotechnical engineering, each adjacent structure during soil liquefaction is conceptualized as a dynamical oscillator (with properties like mass, foundation geometry, and vibration characteristics) linked to others through the common, evolving liquefied soil layer, which acts as a complex, time-dependent coupling medium. The solution involves developing a system of coupled differential equations where the coupling terms represent the interaction forces transmitted through the liquefied soil and the resultant pore pressure feedback. This framework aims to quantify how differential movements emerge and propagate, enabling the prediction of group behaviors such as phase-locked rocking, cascading failure patterns, or beneficial damping effects, thereby providing a novel analytical tool for urban seismic risk assessment of dense building clusters.

ideas.title: Quantified Site-Response Convolution: A Hybrid Physics-ML Framework for Damage Prediction
ideas.description: This research moves beyond the qualitative recognition of site effects to create a predictive, quantifiable model. It develops a hybrid physics-informed machine learning system that convolves regional ground motion attenuation with high-resolution local geotechnical data to produce site-specific damage spectra. The framework integrates convolutional neural networks for spatial soil characterization and recurrent networks for temporal shaking analysis, trained on a global instrumented site database. The resulting model outputs probabilistic damage estimates that explicitly separate and quantify the contributions of source, path, and site effects, providing engineers with actionable predictions for structural design and retrofit prioritization.

ideas.title: Integrate Probabilistic Liquefaction Susceptibility Layers via Epidemiological Risk Mapping Techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from epidemiological risk mapping to the problem of creating detailed soil liquefaction susceptibility maps. In public health, probabilistic risk surfaces for disease are generated by statistically integrating multiple high-resolution spatial determinants. Analogously, for liquefaction, the idea is to move beyond single-parameter zonation by developing a model that fuses diverse, high-resolution data layers—including direct geotechnical measurements, hydrological variables, geomorphic context, and historical performance data—into a unified probabilistic framework. The adapted solution uses an ensemble or Bayesian geostatistical approach, calibrated against a global database of liquefaction manifestations, to weight the contribution of each factor and produce a continuous susceptibility map that directly quantifies spatial uncertainty, thereby significantly improving spatial damage prediction for seismic risk assessment.

ideas.title: Adapting Fracture Mechanics to Model Liquefaction in Heterogeneous Soils
ideas.description: This idea challenges the geotechnical assumption that liquefaction is only a significant problem in deep, uniform soil deposits by drawing an analogy from materials science and fracture mechanics. In that field, the old assumption that cracks propagate only in homogeneous materials was overturned by studying how heterogeneities (like grain boundaries and inclusions) actually control crack initiation and path. The analogous solution involved stochastic models and percolation theory to predict failure in composites. Adapted to geotechnics, this suggests developing new models where native soil variability—such as discontinuous sand lenses, silt seams, and irregular layering—is not seen as inherently stabilizing. Instead, these heterogeneities are analyzed as a complex network that can localize stress, channel pore water pressure, and create connected pathways for liquefaction triggering and propagation. The research would aim to create a framework, potentially using percolation theory or other network-based approaches, to assess liquefaction potential in variable native soils, fundamentally shifting the paradigm from deposit-scale uniformity to the connectivity of liquefiable elements.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Transfer Learning for Liquefaction-Aware Post-Earthquake Pipeline Damage Assessment
ideas.description: This research fuses computer vision-based crack detection with high-resolution soil liquefaction maps to develop a physics-informed deep learning framework. The model uses transfer learning from general crack datasets, but is trained and validated on post-earthquake imagery where each labeled pipe damage instance is co-registered with pre-event liquefaction susceptibility data at its location. This allows the convolutional neural network to learn and internally represent the distinct visual signatures of liquefaction-induced damage (e.g., buoyancy, lateral spreading) versus shaking-induced damage. The resulting system can autonomously assess pipe damage from new imagery while also generating interpretable outputs that attribute damage likelihood to liquefaction potential, significantly improving the accuracy of spatial damage models for infrastructure resilience planning and emergency response.

ideas.title: A Seismic Liquefaction Susceptibility Index (SLSI) via Remote Sensing and Machine Learning
ideas.description: This research adapts the data fusion and predictive modeling approach from precision agriculture to the problem of mapping liquefaction severity. The analogous solution involved integrating remote sensing proxies with ground truth data to create high-resolution predictive models for crop health. Here, we propose to fuse multi-source remote sensing data (e.g., InSAR-derived surface motion, optical/thermal satellite imagery indicating near-surface moisture and soil type) with a spatially distributed database of direct geotechnical measurements (soil composition, state parameters, and water table depth from post-event surveys and existing boreholes). A machine learning model will be trained on this fused dataset to correlate the remote sensing signatures with the ground-measured liquefaction severity. The output will be a high-resolution, spatially continuous map of a Seismic Liquefaction Susceptibility Index (SLSI), providing a detailed probabilistic view of severity and variability across affected areas, effectively interpolating and extrapolating the point-based field data.

ideas.title: Liquefaction as a Networked System: Foundation Design Using Ecosystem Interaction Models
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that foundation design for liquefaction can be done for isolated structures by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, modeling a species in isolation fails because its survival depends on networked interactions (predation, competition) within the broader ecosystem. Similarly, a foundation in liquefiable soil is not isolated; its dynamic response (settlement, pore pressure generation) alters the in-situ stress field for its neighbors, creating a networked system. By adapting ecological network and system dynamics models, we propose to model foundations as interacting agents within a soil matrix. This approach would quantify how liquefaction-triggering and consequences propagate through a foundation group, potentially revealing critical thresholds for cascading failures or emergent group stability that current isolated-design practices cannot predict.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Seismic Field Inference via Physics-Constrained Inversion of Distributed Pipeline Damage
ideas.description: This research develops a robust framework to probabilistically infer the causative ground shaking field by treating a pipeline network as a distributed, albeit fragile, sensor array. It combines observed damage patterns with a physics-based forward model of pipeline fragility that accounts for soil-structure interaction and spatial correlation of ground motion. A Bayesian inversion scheme integrates this with prior constraints from seismic source physics and regional site conditions to resolve the inherent non-uniqueness of the problem. The output is a high-resolution probabilistic shaking map with quantified uncertainty, transforming damage observations into a constrained characterization of the seismic event.

ideas.title: Modeling Pipe Damage with Spatial Correlation via Geostatistical Kriging
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of independent pipe damage events by explicitly modeling their spatial correlation due to ground motion coherence. The analogy is drawn from geostatistics in mining geology, where the spatial correlation of ore grades is solved using variogram analysis and kriging interpolation. The adapted solution involves treating pipe damage intensity as a spatially continuous variable, constructing a variogram from historical event data and seismic coherence metrics to define the correlation structure, and then applying kriging to estimate a correlated damage probability field across the pipeline network. This provides a foundational model for infrastructure risk assessment that replaces independent failure probabilities with a spatially dependent field, significantly improving the accuracy of systemic risk estimates for events like earthquakes.

ideas.title: Apply spatial Hawkes processes from seismology to model pipeline damage clustering
ideas.description: This research adapts the spatial-temporal Hawkes process framework from seismology, where it is used to model earthquake aftershock clustering, to the problem of clustered pipeline damage. In seismology, self-exciting point processes model how mainshocks trigger aftershocks in nearby locations and times through a defined triggering kernel. By transferring this methodology, pipeline damage events can be modeled as a self-exciting process where an initial damage event (e.g., a corrosion pit or external impact) increases the conditional intensity for subsequent damage events in spatially and temporally proximate segments of the pipeline network. This adapted approach would quantify the clustering dynamics, distinguish between primary triggers and secondary cascading failures, and improve risk assessments by predicting spatio-temporal damage clusters based on the self-exciting properties of the infrastructure system.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmacokinetic Dose-Response Models for Seismic Sequence Effects
ideas.description: This idea enhances seismic models by drawing an analogy from pharmacology, where the time-dependent effects of drug doses are critical. The analogous solution uses metrics like Area Under the Curve (AUC) and models for repeated dosing to predict cumulative biological response. Adapted to seismology, the research would develop a framework that treats ground motion as a 'seismic dose,' integrating intensity over duration and across a sequence of events (mainshock-aftershocks). A key innovation is the incorporation of a 'structural recovery function'—akin to a pharmacological half-life—that quantifies how a building's damaged state and increased vulnerability evolve during the quiet period between seismic events, allowing for a physics-informed prediction of cumulative damage from time-dependent ground motion parameters.

ideas.title: Time-Dependent Multi-Utility Resilience Assessment for Mainshock-Aftershock Sequences
ideas.description: This research develops a novel framework to assess the resilience of interdependent regional gas, power, and telecom networks to mainshock-aftershock earthquake sequences. It integrates time-dependent ground motion parameters, such as significant duration and sequence effects, into concurrent multi-utility system models to simulate the evolution of physical damage, functional failures, and restoration processes. This approach reveals how the timing and intensity of aftershocks compound initial disruptions, leading to cascading failures and delayed recovery across interconnected infrastructures. The outcome is a dynamic, scenario-based tool for quantifying regional resilience that accounts for the full temporal profile of seismic hazard, moving beyond static, peak-intensity assessments.

ideas.title: A Causal and Domain-Invariant Framework for Evaluating Seismic Pipeline Damage Models
ideas.description: This research develops a testing protocol that separates a model's ability to learn fundamental damage mechanics from its capture of region-specific correlations. First, a benchmark dataset is constructed by harmonizing pipeline damage reports from multiple seismic events, explicitly annotating confounders like pipe material vintage and local soil conditions. Models are then evaluated not just on raw prediction accuracy per region, but on their performance in a cross-domain adaptation task and their consistency with physics-based counterfactual scenarios. This reveals whether a model generalizes true seismic vulnerability principles or merely memorizes regional data artifacts.

ideas.title: Apply competing risks survival analysis from oncology to model pipe failure modes under seismic shaking
ideas.description: This idea adapts the competing risks survival analysis framework, well-established in clinical oncology for modeling patient outcomes with multiple failure causes, to the problem of pipe network reliability under seismic shaking. In oncology, models use cause-specific hazard functions and cumulative incidence functions to estimate the time to specific events (like cancer-specific mortality) while correctly accounting for competing risks (like death from other causes). For buried pipes, seismic shaking can induce several distinct, mutually exclusive physical failure modes, such as shear failure at joints, crushing from transient soil pressure, or tensile rupture. By adapting the competing risks methodology, we can develop separate cause-specific hazard functions for each failure mode, all conditioned on seismic intensity measures (e.g., Peak Ground Velocity, spectral acceleration). This approach will yield the cumulative incidence probability for each failure type over the duration of shaking, providing a more nuanced and accurate risk profile than a single time-to-any-failure model. The result is a powerful tool for infrastructure managers to identify the most probable failure mechanism for specific pipe materials and burial conditions, enabling prioritized and mechanically targeted seismic retrofit programs.

ideas.title: Multi-Hazard Bayesian Network for Spatially Correlated Pipe Damage
ideas.description: This research develops a probabilistic framework to model the spatial correlation of pipe network damage by integrating multiple correlated hazard and vulnerability factors. A Bayesian network is constructed using spatially indexed nodes for seismic shaking, liquefaction potential, pipe vintage/material, soil properties, and prior failure rates, with conditional probability tables informed by empirical and physics-based models. The system learns the joint probability of damage across the network from this interconnected structure, directly capturing the complex, multi-causal drivers of correlation. This approach provides a more robust and generalizable correlation model for risk assessment and resilience planning under uncertain multi-hazard scenarios.

ideas.title: State-Dependent Seismic Hazard Modeling for Pipeline Networks Using Physics-Informed Survival Analysis
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid framework that overcomes the stationarity limitation of traditional survival analysis for seismic pipe failure. It integrates a mechanics-based degradation model, which calculates a cumulative damage index from specific ground motion records and pipe properties, with a dynamic hazard function where the failure rate is explicitly conditioned on this evolving damage state. The system enables prognostics for remaining useful life after an earthquake and can update failure risk in real-time during aftershock sequences. This results in a more robust, path-dependent reliability assessment tailored to the unique loading history of each seismic event.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Multi-Source Evidence Aggregation for Robust Real-Time Post-Earthquake Impact Estimation
ideas.description: This system moves beyond a single-model prediction from early shaking data to a robust evidence-aggregation framework. It initiates with a lightweight, rapid damage prior from initial seismic waves but immediately begins incorporating crowdsourced human impact reports and triggered automated analysis of readily available low-latency data sources (e.g., social media sentiment, satellite-based power outage detection). These diverse, asynchronous data streams are fused using probabilistic models that explicitly quantify confidence and resolve conflicts, allowing the damage estimate to self-correct and converge on a more accurate and spatially refined assessment as minutes pass, before traditional detailed surveys begin.

ideas.title: Seismic Damage as a Local Resonance Problem: A Bioacoustic Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that shaking intensity alone predicts earthquake damage by proposing an analogy from bioacoustics. In auditory physiology, localized damage in the cochlea occurs due to resonant amplification when sound frequency matches the specific resonant frequency of a region, not just from high overall volume. The analogous solution is adapted to seismology by conceptualizing the urban subsurface (soil columns, water tables, basin geometry) as a complex resonant system with a spatially variable 'frequency response.' The adapted research idea is to shift from a pure attenuation model to a local resonance model. It would develop techniques to create high-resolution maps of a site's resonant 'fingerprint' and correlate severe, localized structural damage to locations where the incoming seismic wave's frequency content critically matches and excites these local resonances, thereby explaining damage outliers that intensity measures alone cannot predict.

ideas.title: Soil perturbation signatures for real-time pipeline network risk reassessment
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to detect and characterize the immediate soil perturbation (e.g., rapid pore pressure change, vibration) caused by a pipeline failure using dense in-situ sensor arrays. These measured 'signatures' are used to calibrate and drive a high-fidelity coupled soil-pipe interaction model in near real-time. The model's output is not the long-term soil effect, but an updated assessment of failure propagation risk and residual capacity for the surrounding buried infrastructure network. This creates a verifiable, observation-driven system for dynamic infrastructure risk management following an initial failure event.

ideas.title: Adapting Functional MRI Analysis to Seismic Tomography for Soil Liquefaction Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from functional neuroimaging to the problem of mapping fine-scale soil variability for liquefaction assessment. In cognitive neuroscience, fMRI uses statistical analysis of time-varying BOLD signals to map dynamic brain function against a static anatomical background. The analogous adaptation applies this principle to seismic tomography. By treating repeated or continuous seismic wavefield measurements as a 4D dataset (3D space + time), we can use pattern recognition and machine learning techniques (like those used to identify brain networks) to isolate and characterize transient subsurface 'functional' states—such as changes in stiffness or damping related to pore pressure dynamics. This moves beyond static imaging to create a dynamic map of soil properties, directly targeting the time-varying conditions that lead to liquefaction.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Operationally-Aware Hydraulic Simulation for Post-Earthquake Water Network Serviceability
ideas.description: This research develops a simulation framework that integrates seismic damage models with a dynamic, adaptive model of post-earthquake network operations. The system explicitly models the sequence of emergency response actions, such as sectional isolation and demand rerouting, that utilities undertake following an event. By generating an ensemble of possible operational states and damage realizations, it produces a probabilistic assessment of flow and pressure serviceability for critical consumers. This approach moves beyond static 'as-built' analysis to provide a realistic, decision-centric evaluation of network resilience and recovery.

ideas.title: Network Component Fragility Modeling via Microstructural Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts a core methodology from materials science—modeling bulk material failure by simulating stress and damage at critical microstructural interfaces like grain boundaries—to the problem of infrastructure resilience. The analogous solution involves shifting from continuum-scale modeling to discrete interface-scale modeling, as seen in crystal plasticity finite element methods. Adapted to civil engineering, this means creating a simulation framework for water or gas distribution networks where the pipe segments are treated as relatively robust 'grains,' and the mechanical joints, welds, and fittings are treated as the vulnerable 'interfaces' or 'boundaries.' The model would define fragility functions and simulate damage propagation (e.g., from seismic waves, ground settlement, or pressure transients) specifically at these component-level connection points, providing a more precise and physically accurate prediction of system-level failure than segment-level models.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Network Resilience Metrics for Post-Earthquake Hydraulic Serviceability
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from ecology and ecosystem science, where network resilience metrics are used to predict functional collapse after disturbances like fires or species extinctions. The analogous solution involves using graph theory on ecological interaction networks (e.g., food webs) to quantify how the loss of nodes or links degrades system-wide functions like energy flow. This is adapted to the context of post-earthquake water system serviceability by modeling the pipe network as a graph where hydraulic flow is the critical function. After simulating seismic damage to pipes and junctions, the research will apply ecological network metrics (e.g., connectivity, centrality, modularity) to the damaged topology. The core adaptation is to correlate these topological resilience scores with results from detailed hydraulic analyses (pressure, flow) to develop simplified, topology-based models that can rapidly estimate serviceability loss across many potential earthquake scenarios, enabling quicker emergency response and prioritization of seismic retrofits.

ideas.title: Mechanics-Constrained Extraction of Causally-Linked Sequence Intensity Measures
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to define and validate time-dependent ground motion parameters for sequences through a closed-loop, mechanics-based process. It uses computational models of fault rupture and wave propagation to generate comprehensive synthetic mainshock-aftershock ground motion suites, explicitly linking source characteristics to the resulting time-series features. A systematic sensitivity analysis then identifies a minimal set of causally linked intensity measures that are both robustly extractable from real records and demonstrably predictive of cumulative structural damage, moving beyond correlation to establish a physics-based justification for their use in performance-based engineering.

ideas.title: Leveraging Sensitivity Analysis from Epidemiology to Test for Omitted Variable Bias in Infrastructure Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from epidemiology—quantitative sensitivity analysis for unmeasured confounders—to the original challenge of testing if omitted factors like traffic load or corrosion are significant confounders in predictive models for infrastructure. In epidemiology, methods like E-value analysis quantify the strength of association an unmeasured variable must have with both exposure and outcome to explain away an observed result. Adapted to this context, the research would formally apply this sensitivity framework to an existing model (e.g., for bridge failure). It would calculate the required strength of correlation between an omitted factor (the confounder), a used predictor variable, and the failure outcome. This yields a quantitative threshold, moving the challenge from a qualitative concern to a testable metric: if plausible real-world values for traffic load or corrosion exceed this threshold, the sufficiency of the original predictors is seriously challenged.

ideas.title: Bayesian Spatio-Causal Inference for Pipeline Risk
ideas.description: This research integrates spatial point process models with Bayesian networks to develop a spatio-causal inference framework for pipeline integrity. The intensity surface from a spatial point process model of historical damage events provides structured spatial evidence, which is fed as an observed node into a Bayesian network encoding hypothesized causal pathways (e.g., soil corrosivity, construction vintage, operational pressure). This allows for the probabilistic quantification of how much each causal factor explains the observed spatial clustering of failures, moving beyond correlation to test causal hypotheses about the drivers of geographic risk patterns.

ideas.title: A Hybrid RR-ML Model with Dynamic Confounder Discovery for Infrastructure Risk
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid Repair Rate (RR) and Machine Learning (ML) model with an integrated, iterative confounder discovery module. The model begins with a standard RR framework but uses ML techniques to spatially disaggregate and refine predictions. Crucially, it systematically tests the significance of candidate omitted variables (e.g., traffic load, corrosion proxies, microclimate data) by evaluating their impact on model performance and residual patterns. This creates a self-improving system where the hybrid architecture's initial predictions guide the search for confounders, and the discovered confounders are then fed back to enhance both the RR and ML components, closing a critical knowledge gap in infrastructure risk assessment.

ideas.title: Seismic Damage Early Warning via Compressed Sensing Inspired by Medical Triage Imaging
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from emergency medical triage imaging, specifically the Focused Assessment with Sonography for Trauma (FAST) scan, to the problem of real-time post-earthquake damage estimation. In medicine, FAST uses a rapid, limited ultrasound protocol to make a critical binary decision about internal injury, sacrificing comprehensive detail for life-saving speed. Analogously, we propose developing a lightweight, rapid-assessment algorithm for seismic damage. This system would ingest only the initial, sparse seismic waveforms (the first tens of seconds of data from a strategically chosen subset of stations) and, using a compressed sensing or machine learning framework trained on historical events, extract key features that correlate with eventual widespread structural damage. The output would be a rapid, coarse-grained damage likelihood map or a binary alert for severe regional impact, enabling emergency services triage within minutes, long before traditional detailed ShakeMaps or inspection reports are available. The core innovation is treating early seismic signals as a compressed 'triage scan' of the built environment's health.

ideas.title: Adapting Oceanographic Data Fusion for Seismic Ground Motion Kriging
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Physical Oceanography, where sparse in-situ measurements are fused with dense remote sensing data using objective analysis techniques like optimal interpolation to create high-resolution 3D field maps. The analogous problem in seismology is predicting Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) with only sparse sensor networks. The adapted research idea proposes to improve PGA interpolation by moving beyond ordinary kriging of sensor data alone. Instead, it employs co-kriging or regression kriging models that integrate sparse, direct PGA measurements with dense, spatially exhaustive secondary variables known to influence ground motion (e.g., topographic slope, VS30 estimates from geology, land cover). This data fusion approach, directly analogous to oceanographic practice, uses the spatial cross-correlation between primary and secondary data to constrain the interpolation, yielding more accurate and physically informed ground motion fields, particularly in data-sparse regions.

ideas.title: Quantifying Multi-Utility Functional Degradation for Cascading Resilience Analysis
ideas.description: This research fuses a continuous severity model for pipeline damage with a multi-utility regional framework to create a novel methodology for resilience analysis. It quantifies damage across gas, power, and telecom networks on a continuous scale of functional degradation (e.g., 10%, 50%, 90% capacity loss). This allows for the simulation of how partial failures in one network propagate as partial stresses to dependent systems, capturing nuanced cascading effects that binary models miss. The outcome is a predictive tool that identifies not just if, but how and at what severity thresholds, regional infrastructure systems degrade toward systemic failure.
