ideas.title: A Causal Bayesian Hybrid Model for Interpretable and Spatially-Accurate Pipeline Risk Assessment
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid pipeline risk model where a Bayesian network serves as the core causal inference engine, explicitly modeling the probabilistic relationships between fundamental factors (e.g., soil corrosivity, pressure cycles, coating quality) and failure. The outputs of this interpretable causal model, including posterior probabilities of latent states like 'active corrosion,' are then used as engineered features for a high-resolution machine learning model (e.g., a spatial Gaussian Process or graph neural network). This architecture leverages the repair rate (RR) model's strength in representing known physical failure mechanisms through the Bayesian network's structure, while the ML component captures complex, localized spatial interactions the causal model alone cannot. The result is a spatially precise risk map that is directly explainable by the underlying causal pathways, providing operators with both accurate predictions and root-cause insights for targeted intervention.

The analogous problem in post-earthquake assessment is the scarcity of expertly labeled imagery for specific pipe damage types. The adapted idea is to employ a transfer learning framework where a vision network is first pre-trained on a broad dataset of infrastructure and material surfaces. It is then fine-tuned using few-shot adaptation techniques on a minimal set of labeled post-earthquake pipe images, enabling accurate autonomous crack and damage labeling despite severe data constraints, mirroring the data-efficiency challenges solved in medical image analysis.

ideas.title: Adaptive Portfolio Optimization for Multi-Hazard Network Resilience
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic simulation-optimization framework for infrastructure retrofit planning that directly addresses cascading failures and multi-hazard uncertainty. It uses an ensemble of probabilistic hazard and damage progression models to stress-test the network and evaluate the system-wide impact of candidate retrofit portfolios over time. The core optimization algorithm identifies not just which assets to upgrade, but the optimal sequence and timing of interventions to maximize the expected resilience pathway of the entire system. This approach ensures that limited budgets are allocated to interventions that provide robust, long-term survivability against interdependent and evolving threats.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporal Probabilistic Degradation Modeling for Pipeline Networks
ideas.description: This research synthesizes variable pipe condition (based on installation year and maintenance) with a continuous damage severity measure to develop a spatiotemporal probabilistic degradation model. The model treats pipeline damage as a time-evolving, continuous field across the network, where the rate of severity progression is conditioned on each pipe's unique historical and material factors. This allows for predicting future network states, identifying segments likely to transition into critical severity bands, and optimizing maintenance by targeting interventions based on predicted severity acceleration rather than just current condition.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Pipe Condition Inference via Multi-Source, Uncertainty-Aware Data Fusion
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to infer pipe condition by probabilistically fusing multiple, incomplete, and uncertain data sources, including installation era, sparse maintenance records, environmental covariates, and historical failure data. Instead of assuming precise knowledge, the model explicitly represents the uncertainty in each input and propagates it through a spatial Bayesian inference process. The output is a spatially explicit condition map with quantified confidence intervals for each pipe segment, enabling robust, risk-informed decision-making for infrastructure management under severe data scarcity.

ideas.title: Structured Causal Discovery and Prioritization of Omitted Variable Threats
ideas.description: This research systematically identifies and prioritizes potentially significant omitted variables, moving beyond a generic checklist. First, it uses a hybrid causal discovery approach combining expert elicitation and algorithmic analysis of existing datasets to map plausible causal pathways and highlight high-impact unmeasured constructs like traffic load dynamics or localized corrosion. Second, it quantifies the potential bias from these prioritized omissions using sensitivity analyses (e.g., E-values, simulation of confounder impacts) to determine which truly threaten inference. This creates a actionable, evidence-based agenda for confounding investigation rather than an open-ended challenge.

ideas.title: Causal Bayesian Networks for Risk-Informed Optimal Retrofit Planning
ideas.description: This research synthesizes causal inference via Bayesian Networks with optimal retrofit planning to develop a novel risk-informed decision framework for pipeline infrastructure. The method constructs a probabilistic graphical model that elucidates the causal relationships between physical degradation, environmental stressors, operational factors, and failure modes. This causal model then informs a dynamic optimization that prioritizes retrofits for pipes that are central to high-probability failure pathways, maximizing the reduction in systemic failure risk per unit cost. The result is a planning tool that not only identifies critical assets but also explains their criticality, allowing for interventions that strategically sever the most dangerous causal chains to network collapse.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Severity-to-Action Framework for Pipeline Integrity Management
ideas.description: This system models pipeline damage on a continuous severity scale but crucially couples it with a decision engine that translates severity into actionable recommendations. The engine dynamically calculates risk-informed intervention thresholds by analyzing the operational context, including potential impact zones, product hazard, and repair crew availability. This provides operators with clear, justified actions instead of just a severity score, directly supporting maintenance planning and emergency response. The result is a robust pipeline integrity management tool that preserves nuanced damage assessment while ensuring operational decisiveness.

ideas.title: Vulnerability-Based Network Immunization for Infrastructure Retrofit
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the network immunization problem from epidemiology to the domain of optimal infrastructure retrofit planning. The analogous problem in public health involves using disease spread models to identify key individuals for vaccination to maximize the reduction in epidemic spread for a limited supply of vaccines. Here, we replace the epidemiological model with a physics-based damage and cascade model for a water distribution network. The solution involves simulating failure scenarios to compute a novel 'systemic vulnerability centrality' metric for each pipe, which integrates its likelihood of failure, its hydraulic criticality, and its role in propagating failures. The optimal retrofit plan is then formulated as a combinatorial optimization problem: select the set of pipes to upgrade within a budget constraint to maximize the reduction in the network's total expected failure impact (e.g., population without service), creating a dual perspective where investment targets the components whose intervention most efficiently increases system-wide survivability.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Model Using a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) to Fuse Repair Rate Theory with Machine Learning
ideas.description: This research proposes a hybrid model that combines the simplicity and theoretical grounding of Repair Rate (RR) models with the spatial accuracy of machine learning (ML), drawing an analogy from Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) in Computational Fluid Dynamics. In CFD, PINNs successfully integrate governing physical equations directly into a neural network's training to ensure physically plausible solutions. Adapting this solution, we will develop a model where the core equations of RR theory are embedded as a soft constraint within a spatial ML model's architecture. The neural network will learn from high-resolution spatial data, but its loss function will include a term that enforces consistency with RR principles. This approach leverages ML's power to capture complex, non-linear spatial dependencies while being anchored and regularized by the interpretable, mechanistic framework of RR, leading to a model that is both accurate and physically meaningful.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Functional-Unit Clustering for Scalable Network Fragility
ideas.description: This approach overcomes computational intractability by grouping similar pipe joints into probabilistically equivalent functional units, each represented by a reduced-order fragility model. System-level risk is assessed using a graph algorithm that simulates failure propagation only along the most vulnerable network pathways, prioritizing high-consequence cascades. This hybrid method preserves the mechanistic insight of component-level analysis while enabling practical simulation of large, real-world water or gas distribution networks. The result is a scalable framework that quantifies system reliability emerging from joint-level fragility without requiring exhaustive enumeration of every connection.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Gated Hierarchical Model with Robust Repair Rate Prior and Residual Machine Learning
ideas.description: This adversarially robust model architecture formally separates robustness from high-resolution accuracy. It employs a Repair Rate (RR) model as a foundational, interpretable layer that provides guaranteed baseline performance. A machine learning component is specifically trained to learn spatial residual corrections to this RR prior. A separate uncertainty quantification module, evaluating both epistemic (model) and aleatoric (data) uncertainty, acts as a gating mechanism. This design ensures the ML enhancement is only activated when reliable, forcing the system to default to the robust RR model in novel, sparse, or out-of-distribution scenarios, thereby eliminating uncontrolled failure modes.

ideas.title: Multi-System Resilience via Coupled Network Epidemiology
ideas.description: This research adapts the metapopulation modeling framework from epidemiology and public health to scale up regional multi-utility risk assessment. The analogous solution involves modeling interacting populations (e.g., humans, animals) with coupled SIR-type models to track disease spread across systems. Here, each critical infrastructure network (gas, power, telecom) is treated as a sub-population with states representing operational health. The interdependencies between these networks—such as a power outage disabling a telecom node, which then hampers gas grid control—are formalized as coupling matrices, analogous to cross-species transmission rates. This adapted approach enables the concurrent simulation of cascading failures across all three utilities, providing a unified model to quantify systemic resilience, pinpoint the most vulnerable interdependency pathways, and evaluate the efficacy of cross-utility mitigation strategies before they are deployed in reality.
